---
title: "Unit 12: In Class Discussion"
author:
  - Elizabeth King
  - Kevin Middleton
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, custom.scss]
    standalone: true
    self-contained: true
    logo: QMLS_Logo.png
    slide-number: true
    show-slide-number: all
code-annotations: hover
bibliography: QMLS_Bibliography.bib
csl: evolution.csl
---


## Course Evaluations Reminder

- Instructor specific (so there are 2 - sorry!)
- Already have an email
- Open until May 7th
- Goal of 100% participation, 25% so far
- Can't find email? 
    - Go to [evaluations.missouri.edu](https://evaluation.missouri.edu/)


## Progress Check 3

- Available Monday
- Review Questions due Wednesday
- Due on May 15th
- Review and Q & A: Friday May 8 10-11:50
    - Optional
    - Recorded

## PS 11 Stretch Question

## GLMs 

> Would it be incorrect to use data prior to log transformation here?  I am assuming interpretation wouldn't change? (image)

> In the salinity example from lecture 12.2, what is one unit for the salinity increase when it is a continuous variable? How is one unit defined for a continuous variable predicting a binomial variable? I understand in the summary how the +/- works, but I don't understand the magnitude.

## GLMs {.smaller}

> Do you have any tips for using the logistic.display function for categorical predictors? I used it on a model, and it converted it over to continous (I assume 0/1). Do I need to just calculate the odds ratio by hand, or is this interpretation ok?

> I got confused when we went over the logit transformation in video 12-2. I also got confused when you were mentioning how odds ratios are reported. I think the example you gave was that if an odds ratio was a high number like 2, it gets reported as something being 200% more likely. Can you explain this a little more?

## PCA

- Several requests to go over PCA & visualizations more

> Can we go over quiz 12-3 question 4? The interpretation of the composite variable. 

## PCA {.smaller}

> Are the composite variables generated by PCA biologically meaningful, or are they just statistical summaries? Also, I am not really sure about why, for q variables, you get q PCs.

> What are some downsides of using PCA to reduce variables, and when would you avoid using it?

> Is there a threshold for a generally "acceptable" PC1 score? Like if it doesn't explain beyond a certain percentage of variance, then a different approach should be utilized?

> For PCA once we have the groupings, how do you start working with the top variables from the first PCA without forgetting that the other pieces exist. You want to reduce variables but also use those variables in some sense since we collected them don't we?


## Dimensionality reduction

> A good [dimensionality reduction analysis] "makes sense"; a bad one does not.

<div class="ref">
Tabachnick and Fidell (2019)
</div>

## PCA

> Is multi-collinearity relatively "accounted-for" in PCA? I've been told that it's important to test for multi-collinearity when doing a PCA, because two variables might over skew the PC loadings or pull from groupings. For example organic carbon and total nitrogen in soil (R2 = 0.95) may overly separate groups within the plot (agriculture vs prairie). 

## Non-independence

> Given that phylogenetic trees are a hypothesized hierarchy themselves, what advice do y'all have for running phylogenetic models with 1. previously published trees and 2. trees you estimate? 

## Today's PS

- First question: No coding or answers from you. Step through, run the code, try to understand, maybe make comments for yourself.
